items.subtopic: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support | PRINCIPLES OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION
items.title: Optimal Tidal Volume for Preventing Ventilator-Induced Lung Injury in ARDS
items.content: In patients with Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS), using a lower tidal volume ventilation strategy is crucial to prevent ventilator-induced lung injury. This approach minimizes barotrauma and volume trauma by keeping the plateau pressure below 30 cmH₂O.
keyPoints: Lower tidal volume ventilation goal: 6 mL/kg of ideal body weight
Plateau pressure should not exceed 30 cmH₂O to minimize lung injury
ARDS patients are particularly susceptible to ventilator-induced lung injury
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support | PRINCIPLES OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support | PRINCIPLES OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION
items.title: Use of Positive End-Expiratory Pressure (PEEP) in Mechanical Ventilation
items.content: Positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) is used to prevent alveolar collapse at the end of exhalation by maintaining a specified positive pressure. This helps optimize lung volumes and prevents cycles of recruitment and collapse.
keyPoints: PEEP maintains positive pressure at end-exhalation
Prevents alveolar collapse, optimizing respiratory system compliance
Helps maintain lung volume along the pressure-volume curve where compliance is greatest
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support | PRINCIPLES OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: MECHANICAL VENTILATION MODES | ASSIST CONTROL VENTILATION
items.title: AC Mode Provides Complete Operator Control Over Ventilation Variables
items.content: Assist control (AC) mode allows clinicians to set and maintain specific ventilation parameters, ensuring minimum minute ventilation. This is crucial for patients unable to participate in their own ventilatory efforts.
keyPoints: AC mode allows complete operator control over variables such as respiratory rate, tidal volume ($ V_{T} $), inspiratory flow rate, PEEP, and $ FiO_{2} $.
In AC volume mode, the clinician sets a specific $ V_{T} $ and respiratory rate to assure minimum minute ventilation ($ V_{E} $).
Patients can trigger additional supported breaths by making an inspiratory effort, which is sensed by the ventilator.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · MECHANICAL VENTILATION MODES | ASSIST CONTROL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: MECHANICAL VENTILATION MODES | ASSIST CONTROL VENTILATION
items.title: Monitoring Inspiratory Pressures in AC Volume Mode to Prevent Barotrauma
items.content: In AC volume mode, the clinician sets tidal volume and respiratory rate, but the end-inhalation pressure is dependent on lung compliance. Monitoring inspiratory pressures is essential to minimize barotrauma.
keyPoints: End-inhalation (plateau) pressure is a dependent variable determined by lung compliance in AC volume mode.
Inspiratory pressures must be monitored to prevent barotrauma due to high pressures.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · MECHANICAL VENTILATION MODES | ASSIST CONTROL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: MECHANICAL VENTILATION MODES | ASSIST CONTROL VENTILATION
items.title: Using PCV Mode to Limit Peak Airway and Lung Distending Pressures in High-Risk Patients
items.content: Pressure control ventilation (PCV) is used to limit peak airway pressures, particularly beneficial in conditions like ARDS or post-thoracic surgery. It sets inspiratory pressure instead of tidal volume, with the resulting tidal volume varying based on lung compliance.
keyPoints: In PCV mode, an inspiratory pressure is set rather than a tidal volume.
Tidal volume varies depending on lung compliance in PCV mode.
PCV helps limit peak airway and lung distending pressures to prevent harm in high-risk conditions like ARDS.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · MECHANICAL VENTILATION MODES | ASSIST CONTROL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: PRESSURE-REGULATED VOLUME CONTROL VENTILATION | PRESSURE SUPPORT VENTILATION
items.title: Understanding PRVC: Balancing Tidal Volume and Patient Effort
items.content: Pressure-regulated volume control (PRVC) ventilation combines the benefits of both volume and pressure control by allowing patient-initiated respiratory efforts while targeting a set tidal volume. It adapts to varying inspiratory flows, enhancing comfort and synchrony but can lead to variable tidal volumes due to non-instantaneous ventilator adaptation.
keyPoints: PRVC allows spontaneous respiratory efforts with target tidal volume settings.
Tidal volumes may vary from breath to breath due to patient effort variability.
Caution is advised in ARDS where tight control of tidal volume is necessary.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · PRESSURE-REGULATED VOLUME CONTROL VENTILATION | PRESSURE SUPPORT VENTILATION

items.subtopic: PRESSURE-REGULATED VOLUME CONTROL VENTILATION | PRESSURE SUPPORT VENTILATION
items.title: Key Aspects of PSV: Monitoring and Patient Triggering
items.content: Pressure support ventilation (PSV) is patient-triggered, with no set mechanical respiratory rate. It requires close monitoring as tidal volume depends on lung compliance and the patient's inspiratory effort. The ventilator supports breathing by maintaining a preset inspiratory pressure until flow decreases below ~20% of peak inspiratory flow.
keyPoints: PSV is entirely patient-triggered with no mandated ventilation rate.
Clinicians set $F_{IO}$, PEEP, and maximum inspiratory pressure.
Tidal volume on PSV depends on lung compliance and patient effort.
Monitoring for hypopnea/apnea and hypoventilation is crucial.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · PRESSURE-REGULATED VOLUME CONTROL VENTILATION | PRESSURE SUPPORT VENTILATION

items.subtopic: NONINVASIVE POSITIVE PRESSURE VENTILATION | ARTERIAL OXYGENATION
items.title: When to Use Noninvasive Ventilation in COPD Exacerbations
items.content: Noninvasive ventilation (NIV) is beneficial for managing moderate acute hypercarbia with a blood pH between 7.25 and 7.35 due to COPD exacerbations, reducing the need for intubation and shortening hospital stays.
keyPoints: Use NIV for COPD exacerbations with blood pH 7.25-7.35
Reduces need for endotracheal intubation
Shortens hospital length of stay
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · NONINVASIVE POSITIVE PRESSURE VENTILATION | ARTERIAL OXYGENATION

items.subtopic: NONINVASIVE POSITIVE PRESSURE VENTILATION | ARTERIAL OXYGENATION
items.title: Optimal Oxygen Targets in Mechanical Ventilation
items.content: There is no clear advantage to conservative oxygen delivery strategies over liberal ones. In ARDS, targeting PaO₂ of 55–70 mmHg (or SpO₂ of 88–92%) did not lower mortality compared to higher targets.
keyPoints: No definitive benefit from supraphysiologic PaO₂ (>100 mmHg)
In ARDS: target PaO₂ 55-70 mmHg or SpO₂ 88-92%
Avoid sustained hyperoxia
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · NONINVASIVE POSITIVE PRESSURE VENTILATION | ARTERIAL OXYGENATION

items.subtopic: NONINVASIVE POSITIVE PRESSURE VENTILATION | ARTERIAL OXYGENATION
items.title: Managing Refractory Hypoxemia in Severe Lung Disease
items.content: For severe acute lung disease like ARDS, if FiO₂ >0.6 or PaO₂:FiO₂ ratio <150 mmHg, consider additional interventions such as PEEP, neuromuscular blockade, prone positioning, and pulmonary vasodilators.
keyPoints: Consider additional interventions if FiO₂ >0.6 or PaO₂:FiO₂ <150 mmHg
PEEP can improve oxygenation by reducing V/Q mismatch
Neuromuscular blockade may be necessary for dyssynchronous respiratory efforts
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · NONINVASIVE POSITIVE PRESSURE VENTILATION | ARTERIAL OXYGENATION

items.subtopic: HYPERCAPNIA
items.title: Mechanisms and Management of Hypercapnia in ARDS
items.content: Hypercapnia is primarily due to inadequate alveolar ventilation, often caused by an increase in dead space relative to minute ventilation. In acute respiratory failure like ARDS, poorly perfused but ventilated lung areas can lead to increased dead space, resulting in hypercapnia. Management involves using higher respiratory rates up to 30 breaths/min and tolerating permissive hypercapnia with a pH as low as 7.2.
keyPoints: Normal physiologic dead space is approximately 150 mL (~2 mL/kg).
Vₑ/Vₔ for a 500-mL tidal volume breath is 0.3.
In ARDS, Vₔ may increase to 200 mL and low Vₑ of 300 mL results in Vₑ/Vₔ of 0.66.
Respiratory acidosis can be managed with higher respiratory rates up to 30 breaths/min.
Permissive hypercapnia is often tolerated down to a pH of 7.2.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · HYPERCAPNIA

items.subtopic: HYPERCAPNIA
items.title: Optimal Ventilatory Strategy for Severe Obstructive Lung Disease
items.content: In severe obstructive lung disease, hypercapnia is common despite mechanical ventilation due to alveolar capillary compression and lung hyperinflation. Optimal management involves using lower respiratory rates (9–12 breaths/min) and moderate tidal volumes (7–9 mL/kg) to maintain minute ventilation of ~10 L/min. Higher minute ventilation can worsen hyperinflation and cause barotrauma.
keyPoints: Average Paco₂ values in severe exacerbations are 65 mmHg with a blood pH of 7.20 after intubation.
Poor alveolar ventilation is due to dead space from alveolar capillary compression.
Increasing minute ventilation paradoxically worsens hypercapnia by increasing gas trapping.
Lower respiratory rates (9–12 breaths/min) and moderate tidal volumes (7–9 mL/kg) are optimal.
Higher minute ventilation usually worsens hyperinflation and can cause barotrauma.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · HYPERCAPNIA

items.subtopic: AIRWAY | ADVERSE CARDIOPULMONARY EFFECTS OF POSITIVE-PRESSURE VENTILATION | VENTILATOR-ASSOCIATED PNEUMONIA
items.title: Risk Factors and Management of Postextubation Stridor (PES)
items.content: Postextubation stridor can occur due to vocal cord injury from prolonged or traumatic intubation, large endotracheal tube size, previous episodes of PES, or head/neck surgery. The cuff leak test helps assess the risk; absence of a cuff leak indicates approximately 30% risk for PES.
keyPoints: Prolonged (>7 days) or traumatic intubation increases PES risk.
Cuff leak test: Absence of leak suggests ~30% risk of PES.
Consider delaying endotracheal tube removal until underlying issues are addressed.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · AIRWAY | ADVERSE CARDIOPULMONARY EFFECTS OF POSITIVE-PRESSURE VENTILATION | VENTILATOR-ASSOCIATED PNEUMONIA

items.subtopic: AIRWAY | ADVERSE CARDIOPULMONARY EFFECTS OF POSITIVE-PRESSURE VENTILATION | VENTILATOR-ASSOCIATED PNEUMONIA
items.title: Cardiopulmonary Effects of Positive-Pressure Ventilation
items.content: Positive-pressure ventilation can exacerbate right ventricular failure and pulmonary arterial hypertension due to increased afterload and decreased preload. It may also cause lung barotrauma with inspiratory plateau pressures >30 cmH₂O or high PEEP.
keyPoints: Inspiratory plateau pressures >30 cmH₂O increase risk of lung barotrauma.
High PEEP can worsen right ventricular failure and pulmonary hypertension.
Consider ventilatory strategy carefully in patients with severe right-sided heart disease.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · AIRWAY | ADVERSE CARDIOPULMONARY EFFECTS OF POSITIVE-PRESSURE VENTILATION | VENTILATOR-ASSOCIATED PNEUMONIA

items.subtopic: AIRWAY | ADVERSE CARDIOPULMONARY EFFECTS OF POSITIVE-PRESSURE VENTILATION | VENTILATOR-ASSOCIATED PNEUMONIA
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia (VAP)
items.content: VAP occurs in up to 15% of ventilated patients, with a mortality rate near 50%. Diagnosis requires new pulmonary opacities on chest x-ray, clinical signs of pneumonia, and positive microbial culture from the lower respiratory tract. Empiric antibiotic therapy should be guided by ICU data.
keyPoints: VAP occurs in ~15% of mechanically ventilated patients; mortality is ~50%.
Diagnosis: New pulmonary opacities, clinical signs, positive lower respiratory tract culture.
Empiric therapy: Intravenous β-lactam with broad gram-negative coverage; adjust based on ICU data.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · AIRWAY | ADVERSE CARDIOPULMONARY EFFECTS OF POSITIVE-PRESSURE VENTILATION | VENTILATOR-ASSOCIATED PNEUMONIA

items.subtopic: OTHER | LIBERATION FROM MECHANICAL VENTILATION
items.title: Minimize Adverse Events in Mechanical Ventilation with Care Bundles
items.content: Implementing care bundles can reduce the risk of extrathoracic complications associated with mechanical ventilation, such as gastrointestinal stress ulcers and deep venous thrombosis.
keyPoints: ICUs should institute care bundles including daily interruption of sedatives and assessment for extubation.
Prophylaxis for deep venous thrombosis is recommended to minimize adverse events.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · OTHER | LIBERATION FROM MECHANICAL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: OTHER | LIBERATION FROM MECHANICAL VENTILATION
items.title: Optimize Ventilator Liberation with Active Assessment and SBT
items.content: Active assessment of a patient's readiness for spontaneous breathing, followed by a Spontaneous Breathing Trial (SBT), can reduce unnecessary mechanical ventilation time.
keyPoints: Criteria for extubation readiness include: $ F_{IO_2} \<= 0.5 $, PEEP < 8 cmH$_2$O, $ S_{AO_2} > 88	ext{%}$, stable hemodynamics, and manageable respiratory secretions.
SBT involves positive pressure set to 5-7 cmH$_2$O for 30 to 120 minutes; successful if $ S_{AO_2} > 90	ext{%}$, respiratory rate <35, systolic BP between 90 and 180 mmHg, heart rate change of <20%.
Patients passing an SBT have a >70% chance of successful extubation.
Incorporating readiness screening and SBT reduces ventilator days by 25% and ICU length of stay by 10%.

Risk factors for failing extubation post-SBT include age >65, congestive heart failure, COPD, APACHE-II score >12, BMI >30, significant secretions, more than two comorbidities, and >7 days on ventilation.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · OTHER | LIBERATION FROM MECHANICAL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support | PRINCIPLES OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION
items.question: Which of the following conditions is most commonly associated with hypoxemic respiratory failure that requires mechanical ventilation?
options: Ventilation-perfusion mismatch or shunt caused by pneumonia
Hypercapnic respiratory failure due to COPD exacerbation
Respiratory muscle weakness from Guillain-Barré syndrome
Poor airway protection in the context of a large upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage
items.explanation: Hypoxemic respiratory failure refractory to supplemental oxygen is often due to ventilation-perfusion mismatch or shunt, commonly caused by pneumonia.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support | PRINCIPLES OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support | PRINCIPLES OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION
items.question: What is the primary goal of using lower tidal volume ventilation in ARDS patients?
options: To increase the work of breathing and strengthen respiratory muscles
To maintain a plateau pressure below 30 cmH₂O to minimize lung injury
To ensure alveolar collapse at end-exhalation for better gas exchange
To reduce positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) requirements
items.explanation: Lower tidal volume ventilation aims to keep the plateau pressure below 30 cmH₂O, minimizing barotrauma and volume trauma-induced lung injury.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support | PRINCIPLES OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support | PRINCIPLES OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION
items.question: What is the purpose of setting a positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) on a ventilator?
options: To prevent alveolar overinflation and barotrauma
To increase tidal volume during mechanical ventilation
To maintain specified positive pressure at end-exhalation and prevent alveolar collapse
To reduce the need for sedation in mechanically ventilated patients
items.explanation: PEEP is set to maintain a specified positive pressure at end-exhalation, preventing alveolar collapse.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support | PRINCIPLES OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: MECHANICAL VENTILATION MODES | ASSIST CONTROL VENTILATION
items.question: In assist control (AC) ventilation mode, what variable is primarily controlled by the clinician?
items.explanation: In AC mode, the clinician controls respiratory rate and tidal volume.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · MECHANICAL VENTILATION MODES | ASSIST CONTROL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: MECHANICAL VENTILATION MODES | ASSIST CONTROL VENTILATION
items.question: What is a potential issue for a dyspneic patient in AC volume control mode?
options: The ventilator will not trigger additional breaths.
The inspiratory flow rate set by the operator may be lower than desired, leading to distress.
The PEEP setting cannot be adjusted during ventilation.
items.explanation: A dyspneic patient may experience resistance if their desired inspiratory flow rate exceeds the set rate.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · MECHANICAL VENTILATION MODES | ASSIST CONTROL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: MECHANICAL VENTILATION MODES | ASSIST CONTROL VENTILATION
items.question: In assist control volume mode, what is a dependent variable?
items.explanation: The end-inhalation (or plateau) pressure is a dependent variable determined by lung compliance.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · MECHANICAL VENTILATION MODES | ASSIST CONTROL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: MECHANICAL VENTILATION MODES | ASSIST CONTROL VENTILATION
items.question: Which statement best describes the difference between AC volume control and PCV?
options: In PCV, tidal volume is set by the clinician.
PCV sets an inspiratory pressure instead of a tidal volume.
AC mode allows patient-controlled respiratory rate in both modes.
PEEP cannot be adjusted in PCV.
items.explanation: In PCV, an inspiratory (driving) pressure is set rather than a tidal volume.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · MECHANICAL VENTILATION MODES | ASSIST CONTROL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: PRESSURE-REGULATED VOLUME CONTROL VENTILATION | PRESSURE SUPPORT VENTILATION
items.question: Which of the following is true about Pressure-regulated Volume Control (PRVC) ventilation?
options: It allows for patient-triggered respiratory efforts and variable inspiratory flow rates.
It mandates a fixed tidal volume with no variability from breath to breath.
It requires a set mechanical respiratory rate similar to PCV.
It is primarily used when patients are less sedated.
items.explanation: PRVC allows patient-triggered efforts and variable inspiratory flow rates, enhancing comfort and synchrony.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · PRESSURE-REGULATED VOLUME CONTROL VENTILATION | PRESSURE SUPPORT VENTILATION

items.subtopic: PRESSURE-REGULATED VOLUME CONTROL VENTILATION | PRESSURE SUPPORT VENTILATION
items.question: In Pressure Support Ventilation (PSV), which of the following is monitored but not assured?
items.explanation: The tidal volume on PSV is determined by lung compliance and patient effort, thus monitored but not assured.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · PRESSURE-REGULATED VOLUME CONTROL VENTILATION | PRESSURE SUPPORT VENTILATION

items.subtopic: PRESSURE-REGULATED VOLUME CONTROL VENTILATION | PRESSURE SUPPORT VENTILATION
items.question: When using Pressure Support Ventilation (PSV), what must be closely monitored to detect hypopnea/apnea and hypoventilation?
options: Tidal volume, minute ventilation, and respiratory rate
items.explanation: Tidal volume, minute ventilation, and respiratory rate must be monitored to detect hypopnea/apnea and hypoventilation in PSV.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · PRESSURE-REGULATED VOLUME CONTROL VENTILATION | PRESSURE SUPPORT VENTILATION

items.explanation: Contraindications for NIV without a secure airway include delirium, difficulty managing secretions, and hemodynamic instability.

options: Requires mechanical ventilation with an endotracheal tube
items.explanation: NIV reduces the need for endotracheal intubation and shortens hospital length of stay in moderate acute hypercarbia due to COPD exacerbations.

items.explanation: If the FiO2 requirement is >0.6 or the PaO2:FiO2 ratio is <150 mmHg, additional interventions should be considered.

Prevents alveolar collapse without side effects

items.explanation: Excess CO₂ production is typically associated with fever, sepsis, and overfeeding, but not obstructive lung disease.

Low respiratory rates and moderate tidal volumes
Increasing minute ventilation by increasing both rate and volume
Deep sedation without adjusting ventilator settings

items.statement: In ARDS patients on mechanical ventilation with no spontaneous respiratory effort, the pressure needed to inflate the lung is different from that required if they were spontaneously breathing.
items.explanation: The amount of positive pressure needed to inflate the lung is equal whether applied positively via mechanical ventilation or negatively using spontaneous breathing.

items.statement: In pressure control ventilation, tidal volume is a controlled variable set by the clinician.

items.statement: Neuromuscular blockade in ARDS improves overall outcomes.

items.explanation: Respiratory acidosis due to permissive hypercapnia can be tolerated down to a pH of 7.2.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · HYPERCAPNIA

items.subtopic: HYPERCAPNIA
items.statement: Increasing minute ventilation by increasing both respiratory rate and tidal volume will always improve alveolar ventilation in obstructive lung disease.
items.explanation: Increasing minute ventilation can worsen hypercapnia by increasing gas trapping and Vₑ/Vₑ in obstructive lung disease.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · HYPERCAPNIA

items.subtopic: AIRWAY | ADVERSE CARDIOPULMONARY EFFECTS OF POSITIVE-PRESSURE VENTILATION | VENTILATOR-ASSOCIATED PNEUMONIA
items.statement: Positive-pressure ventilation can worsen right ventricular failure and pulmonary arterial hypertension.
items.explanation: Positive-pressure ventilation can increase right ventricular afterload, worsening right-sided heart issues.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · AIRWAY | ADVERSE CARDIOPULMONARY EFFECTS OF POSITIVE-PRESSURE VENTILATION | VENTILATOR-ASSOCIATED PNEUMONIA

items.subtopic: AIRWAY | ADVERSE CARDIOPULMONARY EFFECTS OF POSITIVE-PRESSURE VENTILATION | VENTILATOR-ASSOCIATED PNEUMONIA
items.statement: Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) occurs within 48 hours of initiating mechanical ventilation.
items.explanation: VAP is defined as occurring ≥48 hours after the initiation of mechanical ventilation.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · AIRWAY | ADVERSE CARDIOPULMONARY EFFECTS OF POSITIVE-PRESSURE VENTILATION | VENTILATOR-ASSOCIATED PNEUMONIA

items.subtopic: OTHER | LIBERATION FROM MECHANICAL VENTILATION
items.statement: Patients on prolonged mechanical ventilation often develop respiratory muscle weakness.
items.explanation: Respiratory muscle weakness occurs in a minority of patients.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · OTHER | LIBERATION FROM MECHANICAL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: OTHER | LIBERATION FROM MECHANICAL VENTILATION
items.statement: Incorporating extubation readiness screening followed by SBT reduces ventilator days and ICU length of stay.
items.explanation: This approach leads to 25% fewer ventilator days and a 10% decrease in ICU length of stay.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · OTHER | LIBERATION FROM MECHANICAL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: MECHANICAL VENTILATION MODES | ASSIST CONTROL VENTILATION
items.assertion: In assist control ventilation, tidal volume can vary depending on patient effort.
items.reason: The ventilator provides fully supported breaths at the set $V_{T}$ when an inspiratory effort is sensed.
items.explanation: Tidal volume does not vary with patient effort in AC mode; it remains constant as set by the clinician.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · MECHANICAL VENTILATION MODES | ASSIST CONTROL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: MECHANICAL VENTILATION MODES | ASSIST CONTROL VENTILATION
items.assertion: In pressure control ventilation, inspiratory flow rate is a dependent variable.
items.reason: The ventilator adjusts gas flow to maintain the set pressure in response to patient effort.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the inspiratory flow rate varies with patient effort in PCV.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · MECHANICAL VENTILATION MODES | ASSIST CONTROL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: NONINVASIVE POSITIVE PRESSURE VENTILATION | ARTERIAL OXYGENATION
items.assertion: Prone positioning can significantly improve arterial oxygenation in patients with ARDS.
items.reason: Diseased lung is predominantly dependent, and prone positioning improves ventilation/perfusion matching.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · NONINVASIVE POSITIVE PRESSURE VENTILATION | ARTERIAL OXYGENATION

items.subtopic: NONINVASIVE POSITIVE PRESSURE VENTILATION | ARTERIAL OXYGENATION
items.assertion: Sustained hyperoxia has clinical benefits in mechanically ventilated patients.
items.reason: Higher PaO2 levels are associated with better outcomes in some studies.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; there is no evidence that sustained hyperoxia provides clinical benefit.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · NONINVASIVE POSITIVE PRESSURE VENTILATION | ARTERIAL OXYGENATION

items.subtopic: AIRWAY | ADVERSE CARDIOPULMONARY EFFECTS OF POSITIVE-PRESSURE VENTILATION | VENTILATOR-ASSOCIATED PNEUMONIA
items.assertion: Prolonged intubation can lead to tracheal stricture.
items.reason: Tracheal stricture is caused by granulation tissue formation due to prolonged intubation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · AIRWAY | ADVERSE CARDIOPULMONARY EFFECTS OF POSITIVE-PRESSURE VENTILATION | VENTILATOR-ASSOCIATED PNEUMONIA

items.subtopic: AIRWAY | ADVERSE CARDIOPULMONARY EFFECTS OF POSITIVE-PRESSURE VENTILATION | VENTILATOR-ASSOCIATED PNEUMONIA
items.assertion: Hand hygiene before handling the ventilatory circuit is unnecessary for preventing VAP.
items.reason: Hand hygiene reduces bacterial entry into the respiratory tract, lowering VAP risk.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; hand hygiene is a recommended practice to prevent VAP.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · AIRWAY | ADVERSE CARDIOPULMONARY EFFECTS OF POSITIVE-PRESSURE VENTILATION | VENTILATOR-ASSOCIATED PNEUMONIA

items.subtopic: OTHER | LIBERATION FROM MECHANICAL VENTILATION
items.assertion: Overrelying on physiologic variables leads to unnecessary delays in extubation.
items.reason: Successful liberation from mechanical ventilation is better indicated by the outcome of an SBT.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: 313 Mechanical Ventilatory Support · OTHER | LIBERATION FROM MECHANICAL VENTILATION
